Date(s) of Hearing

Toronto, Ontario

May 14, 2012

Gillian Fahy

Conseil(s) du / de la / des demandeur(e)(s) d'asile

N/A
Agent(e) de tribunal

XXXXXXXX
Représentant(e)(s) désigné(e)(s)

N/A
Conseil du ministre
[1] XXXXXXX (hereinafter referred to as the "male claimant") and his spouse, XXXXXX XXXXXXX (hereinafter referred to as the "female claimant"), along with their daughter, XXXXX XXXXX (hereinafter referred to as the "minor claimant"), are citizens of the Czech Republic, claiming refugee protection under sections 96 and 97(1) of the Immigration and Refugee Protection Act (IRPA).1 These claims were joined according to Rule 49(1) of the Refugee Protection Division Rules. The father of the minor claimant was appointed as her Designated Representative according to section 167(2) of the IRPA. The identities of the claimants as nationals of the Czech Republic are established by true certified copies of their Czech passports.2
ALLEGATIONS
[2] A summary of the principal claimant's narrative is as follows:
[3] The principal claimant fears persecution on the grounds of his Roma ethnicity. On one occasion, on his way home, he was attacked and beaten up by a group of skinheads. Although there were onlookers, no one intervened to help him. After they stopped beating him, a Roma man called an ambulance who took him to the hospital right away. In 2004, when he went to buy cigarettes at a convenience store, he was attacked and beaten up by skinheads again. He went to the police to make a complaint and was asked by police if he could identify the skinheads. When the claimant said he did not know the men who attacked him, the police told him there was nothing they could do and sent him home. The claimant left the Czech Republic with his family in XXX of 2009 and came to Canada where they claimed refugee status.
DETERMINATION
[4] I find that the male claimant is not a Convention refugee or a person in need of protection pursuant to sections 96 and 97(1) of the IRPA. The male claimant does not have a well-founded fear of persecution for a Convention reason and his removal to the Czech Republic would not subject him personally to a risk to life, or a risk of cruel and unusual treatment or punishment, or a danger of torture.
[5] In arriving at my decision, I considered the issue of whether the male claimant's fear is objectively reasonable. In this regard, I considered whether or not there is adequate state protection in the Czech Republic, whether or not the male claimant took all reasonable steps to avail himself of that protection, and whether he has provided clear and convincing evidence of the state's inability to protect him.
ANALYSIS
Section 96 - Convention Refugee Grounds
State Protection
[6] In assessing the issue of state protection, I am guided by a number of cases from the Supreme Court of Canada and the Federal Court. In this regard, the courts have established a number of principles relating to state protection and I have applied those principles to the case at hand.
[7] There is a presumption that a state is capable of protecting its citizens except in situations where the state is in a state of a complete breakdown.3 To rebut the presumption of state protection, a claimant must provide clear and convincing evidence of the state's inability to protect, absent an admission by the national's state of its inability to protect that national.4 While the effectiveness of the protection is a relevant consideration, the test is whether the protection offered is adequate.5 The evidence that state protection is not adequate must be reliable and probative and it must also satisfy me, on a balance of probabilities, that the state protection is inadequate.6 The claimant must approach the state for protection, providing that state protection might be reasonably forthcoming.7 In the absence of a compelling explanation, a failure to pursue state protection opportunities within the home state will usually be fatal to a refugee claim, at least where the state is a functioning democracy with a willingness and the apparatus necessary to provide a measure of protection to its citizens.8 Where a state is in effective control of its territory, has military, police and civil authority in place and makes serious efforts to protect its citizens, the mere fact it is not always successful at doing so will not rebut the presumption of state protection.9 A claimant cannot rebut the presumption of state protection in a functioning democracy by asserting only a subjective reluctance to engage the state.10 Doubting the effectiveness of the protection offered by the state when one has not really tested it does not rebut the existence of a presumption of state protection.11 The fact that state protection is not perfect12 does not constitute clear and convincing proof of the state's inability to protect its citizens, since no state can guarantee the protection of all its citizens at all times. Local failures to provide effective policing do not amount to a lack of state protection unless they are part of a broader pattern of the state's inability to provide protection.13 Indeed, if a claimant believes the actions of some police officers are corrupt, the onus is on them to approach other members of the security forces or authorities.14 There is nothing in the documentation before the Board to suggest that the Czech Republic is in a state of complete breakdown. The burden of proof that rests on the claimant increases with the level of democracy of the state in question. The more democratic a state is, the more the claimant must have done to exhaust all courses of action open to them to demonstrate state protection was or would not be forthcoming.15 The Czech Republic is a multiparty democracy16 Therefore, the presumption of state protection is a strong one. The claimant must do more than simply show that he or she went to see some members of the police force and that his or her efforts were unsuccessful.17 Even where the protective services of the home state have gaps or deficiencies, a refugee claimant who alleges a subjective fear based on criminality must, in the absence of a compelling justification, take reasonable steps to access those services.18 Where there is evidence that the government is taking steps to protect the target group, in the absence of evidence to the contrary, it must be presumed that these steps will be effective.19 It is not enough that a government is willing to provide protection and is making serious efforts to do so. In order for state protection to be present, the efforts made must adequately protect citizens in practice.20 In order for adequate state protection to exist, a government must have both the will and capacity to implement effectively its legislation and programs.21
Review of Country Documentation on the Roma in the Czech Republic
Refugee Board Documents (Exhibit R-1, NDP April 13, 2011)
Country Reports on Human Rights Practices for 2010
[8] In the United States Department of State Report22 covering the period of 2010 in the Czech Republic, it states:
...Notable human rights problems included official corruption, trafficking of persons for commercial sexual exploitation and labor, neo-Nazi and nationalist extremism directed at Roma and other minorities, and societal discrimination against Roma....Salary cuts at the Ministry of the Interior following government-wide austerity measures resulted in a lowered rate of retention among the national police force, especially among experienced detectives, which hampered the effectiveness of their work. Corruption remained a problem among law enforcement bodies. The Ministry of the Interior oversees police actions and is responsible for investigating allegations of police misconduct. Observers believed that the ministry sometimes whitewashed wrongdoing or prematurely terminated investigations of units under its control...The law mandates prison sentences of six months to three years for persons who deny Communist-era crimes or the Nazi Holocaust. Speech inciting hatred based on race, religion, class, nationality, or other group affiliation is also illegal and carries a sentence of up to three years in prison. In June eight members of the National Resistance, a neo-Nazi group, were indicted in Prague on charges of supporting and promoting Nazism. The individuals posted propaganda materials in public places and organized neo-Nazi events. The defendants each faced multiple counts with a possible total of eight years in prison if convicted. The trial was postponed, and two defendants, who had been held without bail since October 2009, were released on bail in December. In June an administrator of iDNES.cz news server filed criminal charges against a contributor nicknamed "Kraxna" for threats against his life and family. Kraxna had frequently cursed and threatened the administrator for removing racist comments from group boards. The administrator filed the charges after the contributor threatened to kill his son and mentioned the son by name, heightening concern over the threat. Police investigated the case and arrested Kraxna for committing violence against a group or individual members of a group. The offender confessed to the crime and promised not to commit the crime again in the future. As a result, Kraxna received a six-month suspended sentence....The law provides criminal penalties for official corruption. However, the government did not always implement the law effectively, and officials sometimes engaged in corrupt practices with impunity. According to World Bank governance indicators, corruption was a problem in the country...Several ministries have anticorruption hotlines for citizens to report allegations. The Ministry of the Interior's anticorruption hotline, administered by the country branch of the NGO Transparency International (TI), received 4, 653 calls in the first six months of the year. TI registered 400 new client and identified 290 corruption complaints, 67 concerning the judiciary, 41 concerning the police, 79 concerning property rights and construction permits, and 43 concerning public procurement. Almost one-third of the complaints (122) concerned municipal officials....The law prohibits discrimination based on gender, age, disability, race, ethnic origin, nationality, sexual orientation, religious faith, or personal belief. However, the government did not effectively enforce these provisions, and significant societal discrimination against Roma and women persisted....Roma faced high levels of poverty, unemployment, and illiteracy as well as varying levels of discrimination in education, employment, and housing. Societal prejudice against the country's Romani population occasionally manifested itself in violence. Members and sympathizers of neo-Nazi organizations were the most frequent perpetrators of acts of interethnic violence, particularly against Roma....During the year neo-Nazi and skinhead rallies or marches took place in several cities. On March 14, a Molotov cocktail was thrown into a bedroom of a Romani home in the settlement of Bedriska. A 14-year old girl was asleep in the room at the time. The cocktail failed to explode upon impact, resulting in no injuries and little damage to the home. Local police arrested a neighbor and her minor son on charges of attempted murder...On December 10, the regional court in Ostrava sentenced the boy to a three-year suspended sentence for attempted reckless endangerment and his mother to an 18-month suspended sentence for not stopping he son from throwing the cocktail. On May 12, the regional court in Ostrava opened the trial against Jaromir Lukes, David Vaculik, Ivo Mueller, and Vaclav Cojocaru, who were accused of throwing Molotov cocktails into the home of a Romani family in the town of Vitkov in April 2009. Three persons, including a two-year-old girl who was treated for second-and third-degree burns over 80 percent of her body, were injured in the resulting fire. On October 20, the court found all four guilty of attempted murder and property damage. The judge found the crime to involve extraordinary circumstances that allowed for more stringent sentencing. Vaculik, Mueller, and Lukes received 22-year sentence, and Cojocaru received a 20-year sentence. These are the longest sentences ever handed down for a racially motivated crime. On November 27, Pavel Louda, mayor of Novy Bydzov, posted a statement on the city's official Web site declaring a "war on gypsies". In the statement he collectively blamed all Roma for rapes and thefts in the town and declared that the citizens wanted the Roma to "disappear". He threatened to undertake repressive measures in order to bring the Roma under control. The prime minister and other government officials released statements condemning the mayor's actions. In December police opened an investigation of a young Czech man for promoting Nazism on Facebook...Police arrested a number of right-wing extremists during 2009. In October 2009 UOOZ, the police Unit for Combating Organized Crime, arrested 24 individuals and charged 18 with supporting and promoting movements aimed at suppressing the rights and freedoms of the individual. Police president Oldrich Martinu stated that several of those arrested were linked to the neo-Nazi organization White Justice and had been trained by Lukas Sedlacek, a member of the Czech military....Other arrestees were linked to the National Resistance, the Workers Party, and the Resistance Women Unity group. In June the district court of Benesov found 11 of the defendants guilty, sentencing two individuals to 30 months' imprisonment, one individual to two year's imprisonment, two teenagers to one year in a juvenile detention center and the others to a 30-month suspended sentence....During the year eight men stood trial for a series of attacks on Roma in 2008 in Sumbark, a neighbourhood in Havirov with a large Romani population. Some of the alleged attackers were described as members of the group Thugs Havirov and one as a member of the group National Resistance. One attack victim was severely beaten. On December 17, the regional court in Ostrava found the defendants guilty; sentencing was deferred until the sentence for one of the defendants in an unrelated assault case was processed. In its 2009 annual report on hate crimes, the Organization for Security and Cooperation in Europe (OSCE) reported that 188 persons were prosecuted and 103 persons were sentenced for crimes motivated by racial or similar hatred. Over the past two years, police focused greater attention on combating promoters of extremism. The Security Information Service (BIS), the country's civilian domestic intelligence agency, reported at the start of May that right-wing extremist activity decreased in the country during the first six months of the year. BIS stated that developments involving neo-Nazis were particularly influenced by police raids in 2009 against members of extremist parties and the prohibition of the Workers' Party....The law prohibits employment discrimination based on ethnicity; however, Roma continued to face discrimination in employment...Roma faced widespread discrimination in access to housing and other accommodations....
Czech Republic Fact Finding Mission Reports-June-July-2009
[9] The Research Directorate of the IRB undertook a fact-finding mission to the Czech Republic from March 23 to March 31 of 2009. The purpose of the mission was to address information gaps related to the situation and treatment of the Czech Roma as well as their access to state protection and the possibility of internal relocation. The Research Directorate produced two Issue Papers reporting its findings, one dated June of 2009 and the other in July of 2009. The Research Directorate met with government officials, representatives of human rights organizations, Romani groups and academics based in Prague, Brno, Chomutov, Rokycany and Usti nad Labem.23
According to the Report of the Research Directorate, dated June of 2009:
Interlocutors from governmental and non-governmental organizations (NGO) explained that an estimated 80,000 of the country's 200,000 to 250,000 Roma inhabit some 300 so-called "socially excluded localities". Social excluded localities are residential enclaves mainly populated by Roma that have limited access to formal employment, education, and adequate housing....in several unspecified cases, excluded localities have been "neglected" by local authorities, including the police. Academic, governmental and NGO interlocutors claimed that the crime rate in these areas is considerably above the Czech average...interlocutors referred to legislation that provides for state protection for Roma, in particular, anti-discrimination and hate-crime legislation. The Czech Constitution prohibits discrimination on the basis of national, racial or ethnic background, as do other pieces of legislation, including laws governing employment and education, and the Charter of Fundamental Rights and Freedoms. The Security Police Department of the Ministry of the Interior and the Czech Helsinki Committee noted that Czech law outlines a number of provisions defining hate crimes....when queried about police protection measures established by the state to address acts of discrimination and racially motivated violence, governmental interlocutors pointed to Roma Police Assistants (RPAs), Minority Liaison Officers, and the Ministry of Interior's Anti-Extremism Strategy. The Roma Police Assistant (RPA) program was created in 2003 with seed money from the Ministry of Labour and Social Affairs, in the eastern city of Ostrava where the first two RPAs aided the police in an investigation into allegations of usury. RPA's are now mainly funded by municipalities via national government grants. As of March 2009, seven RPAs were operating in municipalities across the country, and an eighth position was planned for the Janov neighbourhood of Litvinov. RPA duties include establishing contact between Roma and the Police, accompanying Romani victims of crime who wish to file a police report, educating victims on their legal rights and responsibilities, helping police investigate unreported crime and assisting in the provision of social services in socially excluded localities....Several NGO interlocutors also identified the existence of the RPA program as a sign of progress and two interlocutors claimed that RPAs have increased Romani trust in the police....Holomek and Brno-based NGO IQ Roma Servis agreed that RPAs have improved relations between Roma and police, but stated that RPAs are sometimes perceived as informants seeking information about criminal activity in Romani communities....Minority Liaison Officers are employed by the Ministry of the Interior to help introduce police to minority groups and to communicate with NGOs and local authorities with a focus on crime prevention. MLOs also participate in projects intended to combat the social exclusion of Roma. The hiring of MLOs began in 2004 in areas with a high concentration of Roma, such as Litvinov. In March 2009, eight MLOs were operating in the Czech Republic at the regional level. Hule, an NGO with several regional branches that fights against poverty and social exclusion, claimed that MLOs are overextended due to the size of their jurisdictions. Nevertheless, Hule also noted that the MLO in Prague was in regular contact with the Ministry of the Interior requesting information about cases involving Romani victims of violence. Two NGO interlocutors commended the effectiveness of MLOs, and the Ministry of the Interior stated that MLO activities had improved communication between police and Roma, contributing to a reduction of crime within Romani communities....In the mid-1990s, the Ministry of the Interior launched an initiative to combat extremism, allowing for police monitoring of extremist group activities and trends. The initiative began with the creation of a specialized anti-extremist department within the Police Presidium of the Czech Republic in 1995, followed by the deployment of anti-extremist specialists at the regional and district level in 1996. There are between 130 and 140 extremist crime investigators across the country....According to the Ministry of the Interior, anti-extremist measures have shown increasing signs of success, for instance, the number of extremist crimes declined from 473 in 2002 to 196 in 2007. However, Vales of the Czech Helsinki Committee described extremist crime investigators as "overburdened with work", and also noted that there is limited coordination between anti-extremism agencies and local police, the latter of which he viewed as "inefficient and unprofessional"....According to Holomek of the Romani Association of the Romanies in Moravia and to Miroslav Mares, a professor of political science at Masaryk University in Brno, police response to racially motivated demonstrations targeting Roma is generally adequate. Holomek noted that in May 2008, police successfully prevented a major clash between anarchists and neo-Nazis in Brno, expelling the latter from the city. Governmental and NGO interlocutors also described the police response to the November 2008 Workers' Party march in Litvinov as satisfactory and noted that the police were able to break up the demonstrations. Vales, of the Czech Helsinki Committee, described the police response to neo-Nazi demonstrations as inadequate. For example, he believed that violence at the November 2008 Litvinov demonstration was averted "by chance" rather than organized police efforts. Other NGO interlocutors claimed that the police tended to address acts of extremism only when they were considered serious or too high-profile to ignore....Governmental and NGO interlocutors varied in their assessment of police relations with Roma. Police in Brno pointed to high levels of crime and an uncooperative attitude with the police in Roma communities as deterrents in fostering better interactions between police and Roma....In contrast, several NGO and academic interlocutors commented that the police generally have a negative view of Roma that sometimes includes "discriminatory" or "disrespectful attitudes. Several governmental interlocutors claimed that, in general, the police response to Roma complaints or calls of distress is equal to that of non-Romani Czechs. In addition, Mares and Holomek stated that the police response to racially motivated crime against Roma is generally adequate. However, other NGO interlocutors were more cautious in their assessment stating that despite some signs of progress, negative perceptions of the police persist among Roma. A number of NGO interlocutors asserted that Roma are often hesitant to file criminal complaints because they lack confidence in the police and doubt that their complaints will be effectively addressed. Zvule prava, a Prague-based civic association advocating for Romani rights, noted that Roma generally believe that the police do not take their cases seriously. Roma are said to report crimes to the police only in the most serious circumstances, such as when they believe that they are in imminent physical danger...Some interlocutors also claimed that the police have been known to dissuade Roma, especially those from socially excluded localities, from filing a complaint....NGOs and academic interlocutors noted that various ministries differ in their approaches to address social exclusion and that there is a lack of coordination. In an effort to resolve these issues, the Social Inclusion Agency was established by the national government in January of 2008. The objectives of the Agency are to coordinate social integration efforts, combat social exclusion, evaluate social integration programs, monitor cases of discrimination, provide legal advice, and promote education and employment at the local level. In March of 2009, the Agency was active in 12 cities as part of its pilot phase and is expected to take on a nationwide mandate by 2010. IQ Roma Servis believed the Agency is hampered by poor management....
According to the Report of the Research Directorate, dated July of 2009:
Karel Holomek, Chairperson of the Association of Romanies of moravia and Zuzana Kailova of Usti and Labem City Hall indicated that while the police and government are working on curbing open discrimination against Roma, a more pervasive problem is hidden discrimination, which is difficult to tackle directly. Several interlocutors cited public opinion surveys which found that between 60 and 95 percent of Czech citizens held negative attitudes toward Roma. Miroslav Mares explained that political extremism in the Czech Republic is weak in comparison with some neighbouring countries. Despite negative public perceptions of Roma, he asserted, extremist political parties remain unsuccessful in attracting sufficient support to win elections...However, interlocutors asserted that some mainstream national parties occasionally espouse platforms considered anti-Roma, especially during elections. In addition, several interlocutors indicated that discrimination manifests itself politically at the local level. For example, they pointed to municipal politicians who have adopted policies perceived as anti-Roma in order to gain votes. According to interlocutors from IQ Roma Servis, local governments that adopt policies to address Romani concerns are often criticized by their non-Roma constituents, who view these policies as ineffective.....Mares indicated that while there has been increased visibility of extremist activities, such as demonstrations, in the past five years, he reported that it was not clear if such activism has resulted in increased violence against Roma. He maintained that, as of March 2009, extremist attacks against Roma were low compared to earlier in the decade....According to the SPD of the Ministry of the Interior, violent crimes committed in an extremist context such as homicide, attempted homicide or assault resulting in serious injury, have occurred, but are uncommon...Frantisek Vales, Executive Director of the Prague-based Czech Helsinki Committee, state that while official statistics recorded no racial murders between 2002 and 2006, three neo-Nazis killed a Romani person in 2006. The three men were prosecuted for non-racially motivated murder, and one of them was sentenced to a prison term. Vales went on to say that in 2007 there were 11 crimes resulting in the physical harm of Roma as well as one murder which was attributed to the victim's socio-racial background. The perpetrator of the killing was charged with murder and hooliganism, but these were not classified as hate crimes...When questioned about particular groups that target Roma, interlocutors explained that far-right wing extremist groups have instigated "hate" crimes, not only against Roma, but also against other minority groups. NGO and government interlocutors described physical attacks targeting Roma, particularly those resulting in serious injury, as not prevalent. For instance, when queried about the extent of anti-Romani activism by extremist groups, Mares indicated that the Roma do not face significant physical danger from such groups in Prague. Cerny of People in Need's Usti nad Labem office observed that, despite vocal extremist agitation, Roma living in the Janov district of Litvinov do not suffer from physical violence...A 2008 report provided by the Ministry of the Interior's SPD indicated that Roma are the most frequent victims of verbal and physical assaults by extremists, followed by "foreigners of dark skin", persons of Asian origin and other foreign nationals, though drug users, homosexuals and homeless people also face threats....Extremist groups are engaged in various activities, including organizing meetings and concerts and publishing manifestos and promotional material. However, the extremist activity most frequently cited by interlocutors was public demonstrations. Interlocutors reported an increase in the mobilization of anti-Roma extremist groups in recent years, as exemplified by the rise of the Worker's party in northern Bohemia.....
Response to Information Request number CZE02961.E, December 16, 2008
[10] According to the above Response to Information Request,24 it states:
....Although there are few statistics available regarding police treatment of Roma, Freedom House's Nations in Transit 2008 report for the Czech Republic indicates that "violent attacks on foreigners and the Roma minority have declined since the 1990s and remain largely out of the headlines". A Czech Ministry of the Interior report entitled Information on the Issue of Extremism in the Czech Republic in 2006, notes that "Romas, as the most visible minority, prevailed among victims of verbal and physical assaults, followed by foreigners of dark skin, persons of Asian origin, or other foreign nationals"....In October of 2008, a representative of the ERRC stated: " that if a Roma individual is able to access legal aid-which would have to be through the handful of Czech NGOs that offer such services free of charge-and if they are persistent, they may eventually see justice done in their case, but verdicts are not consistent. Even brutal violence is often met with suspended sentences, especially if the perpetrators are considered "juvenile". For crimes alleged to have been committed by police, a key factor in such cases is the lack of independent oversight of criminal investigations of the police themselves." According to Amnesty International's 2008 report for the Czech Republic, the Committee on the Elimination of Racial Discrimination and the European Committee for the Prevention of Torture raised concerns about allegations of ill-treatment and excessive use of force by police offices, in particular against Roma....In July, the Supreme Court upheld a two-year prison sentence on a former Brno police officer for blackmail and misuse of power by law enforcement officials against a 14-year-old Romani boy. Reports of police misconduct continued, particularly against Roma and other marginalized groups, especially at the time of arrest and detention...
News Article-Romea.cz-Prague, November 18, 2009
[11] In the above internet news article,25 it states:
Neo-Nazis who attempted to disrupt the celebrations of the 17 November anniversary in the centre of Prague by holding two unannounced demonstrations yesterday afternoon did not succeed. About 300 right-wing extremists had their plans spoiled by police, who kept them away from the reenactment of the march held 20 years ago between Albertov and Narodni trida. The neo-Nazis did succeed in attacking three random people, they also threw stones and glass bottles at the police and kicked their vehicles. There were 48 arrests...The situation is being monitored by riot police, who are flanking and following the parade as it heads for Narodni trida....15 police vans have arrived at the Vyton tram stop, where they will take those arrested away to police stations...After a few moments there were clashes with police, who are throwing firecrackers into the crowd....About 70 riot police distributed in several rows are overseeing the event, which is also being monitored by a police helicopter from the air...
Amnesty International Report 2010 on the Czech Republic
[12] In its 2010 Report,26 Amnesty International stated:
...Roma faced increasingly overt public hostility, as well as segregation in schools and housing and discrimination in employment. In March the Supreme Administrative Court, citing insufficient evidence, rejected a government proposal to dissolve the far-right Workers' Party, which organized vigilante patrols targeting Roma. The European Commission against Racism and Intolerance (ECRI) expressed concern in a report in September at mounting anti-Roma hate speech in public discourse and at repeated demonstrations by extreme right-wing groups. It recommended vigorous implementation of laws prohibiting racist violence and incitement to hatred. On April 4, far-right groups organized a march through the Romani district in the town of Prerov. The Worker's Party, which initially announced the march, later distanced itself from the event. About 500 demonstrators, chanting anti-Roma slogans and joined by local inhabitants, marched through the town and the Romani neighbourhood. Around 700 police officers prevented direct attacks on Roma, but violence later broke out as demonstrators attacked riot and mounted police...
Report on the Framework Convention for the Protection of National Minorities
[13] In its third period report,27 on measures taken to give effect to the principles set out in the Framework Convention for the Protection of National Minorities, in accordance with the resolution for the Protection of National Minorities by the Czech Republic, adopted by the committee of Ministers on March 15, 2006, representatives of the Czech Republic stated:
...The periodic report covers the period between 2004 and 2008....In 2007, the Czech Republic completed the second monitoring cycle of the Convention. The cycle was concluded with a Follow-up workshop on the implementation of the Framework Convention in Prague on October 15, 2007, organized by the National Minorities Council in cooperation with the Convention Secretariat....In conclusion, the workshop noted that the dialogue was well under way and that the Czech Republic demonstrated a genuine strategic commitment and resolute approach to the implementation of the Convention. The main problem identified at the workshop was insufficient communication between the central government and local governments. The workshop appreciated that the Czech government policy on national minorities had remained stable over many years, throughout a succession of governments formed by different parties...After the passage in the Chamber of Deputies and the Senate, the draft Anti-discrimination Act was vetoed by the President of the Republic in May 2008 and returned to the Chamber of Deputies for a new debate. The Act was finally adopted by the Chamber of Deputies on June 17, 2009 and entered into force on September 1, 2009. In its policy statement of January of 2007, the Government undertook to create an agency to provide comprehensive services aimed at preventing and eliminating social exclusion in Roma locations. The aim was to streamline the use of EU funds for activities promoting the integration of socially excluded Roma population. The Government sought to use the unique opportunity to benefit from the EU structural funds at the start of the new year 2007-2013 programming period in order to establish and staff an institution providing support to programmes and projects designed to bring about radical improvement of the social and economic conditions of the Roma, including their standard of living and their access to the job market and mainstream education....Since January 2008, the Agency has been gradually starting to work in twelve selected towns and municipalities where it is to implement, in cooperation with local governments, a programme addressing the situation in excluded locations on a comprehensive basis. The municipalities will work with their partners( non-governmental non-profit organizations, schools, Employment Centres, local employers, Roma community) on projects to encourage employment and education and to improve housing conditions for people in socially excluded Roma locations....activities undertaken in the period under review by the Czech Republic Police under its strategy of work with national minorities included; Minority liaison officers and their working groups at Regional Headquarters of the Czech Republic Police continued to develop their activities; The project of Czech Republic Police Assistants working in socially excluded locations was further broadened. The aim is to provide support to crime victims in socially excluded locations and to more efficiently detect latent crime in such locations. At present, the project is under way in Ostrava, Cheb, Brno, Karvina and Pilsen; The Czech Republic Police City Headquarters in Brno has completed a one-year pilot project testing the uses of supervision as a personnel management tool in the Czech Republic Police; the work on surveys mapping crime in socially excluded locations continued....
Response to Information Request number CZE103682.E, March 1, 2011
[14] In the above Response it states:28
A European Network Against Racism (ENAR) shadow report on racism and discrimination in the Czech Republic describes police investigation of crimes against Roma citizens as "inadequate", and adds that such investigations are "presumed" to be the result of "ethnic profiling". The report, which the Research Directorate obtained in advance of its March 21, 2011 publication date, also describes the police reaction as a form of "secondary victimization" stemming from the "assumption that the Roma committed the criminal act or provoked it with their behaviour". As the ENAR explains, the phenomenon is "particularly prominent in cases of hate crimes; for example, many arson attacks on Roma families were not investigated or the investigation was adjourned." However, the Embassy of the Czech Republic in Ottawa, in February 2011 correspondence with the Research Directorate, counters that "ethnicity...has no effect on the quality of the services rendered by the Police of the Czech Republic: all are treated equally, without discrimination". The Embassy of the Czech Republic acknowledges that although there can be "individual cases of failure to comply" with its standards, the police "have the power, in such cases, to employ means that identify and punish discriminatory conduct of police officers". The Inspection of the Police of the Czech Republic, an independent body under the Minister of the Interior undertakes the criminal investigation of police officers, reports the Embassy of the Czech Republic. Citizens can also register "complaints about police inaction and non-serious offenses by police officers" with the Office of the Internal Control, which is part of the Police of the Czech Republic. Information on the Police of the Czech Republic website also states that complaints about the police force generally or individual policemen in particular are to be sent to what it translates as the "Office of Internal Inspection". Citizens do so by sending an email or calling. The complaints will either be immediately addressed or "submitted for inspection" to the responsible bodies. Liga Lidskych Prav, a human rights non-governmental organization (NGO) in the Czech Republic points out that there is no "single, independent police complaints commission" that can investigate all types of complaints against police, including accusations of misconduct or criminal charges against the police. Instead, these types of complaints "are investigated by the police's own internal monitoring body"; and the Interior Minister's Inspectorate, which is "exclusively made-up of police officers, all of whom still retain their rank," determines "the most important stage of the investigation"-whether a crime was actually committed...According to the Police of the Czech Republic, citizens who wish to submit a complaint against the police should report the complaint to the director of the unit "who is the supervisor of the members or staff of the Police you are filing y our complaint against" and report the complaint to thee supervising unit of the director does not address it appropriately. If the complaint is submitted to a unit that is not responsible, the unit will pass the complaint to the responsible unit and inform the complainant...
News Article from Romea.cz, dated November 18, 2009
[15] In the above news article29 it states:
Neo-Nazis who attempted to disrupt the celebrations of the 17 November anniversary in the center of Prague by holding two unannounced demonstrations yesterday afternoon did not succeed. About 300 right-wing extremists had their plans spoiled by police, who kept them away from the reenactment of the march held 20 years ago between Albertov and Narodni trida. The neo-Nazis did succeed in attacking three random people; they also threw stones and glass bottles at the police and kicked their vehicles. There were 48 arrests...
Response to Information Request number CZE103684.E, March 10, 2011
[16] In the above Response30 it states:
In the lead-up to becoming a European Union member country in May 2004, the Czech Republic, under pressure to "protect the human rights of the Roma and improve their condition within society, "implemented a number of laws and policies to "address persistent systemic discrimination against the Roma". However, a senior research fellow at the Norwegian Institute of International Affairs (NUPI), writing in a November 2010 publication for the Cetnre for European Policy Studies (CEPS), questions the effectiveness of those measures". Since the Czech Republic's accession into the EU, the government has continued to develop institutional and policy measures to address the situation of the Roma. The Council of Europe's Commissioner for Human rights, reporting on the results of interviews with government authorities and representatives of civil society during his visit to the Czech Republic in November 2010, commends the Czech Republic for implementing Roma inclusion strategies "for many years now" although he points out that the government needs to "monitor outcomes through the collection of adequate statistical data" to determine their effectiveness "on the ground"....The agency for Social Inclusion is responsible for "coordinating and improving social integration efforts with respect to Roma". The Czech Republic reported that the Agency supports local authorities in integrating Roma residents "threatened with social exclusion" and in developing socially excluded localities within the town or village as a whole. In 2009, the Agency "worked within local partnerships with 139 institutions from 13 pilot locations", and in March 2010, added another 10 locations. As of August 2010, the Agency had "worked with 269 local partners on developing and implementing local strategies for social integration. The Council of Europe's Human Rights Commissioner similarly notes in his report that the Agency for Social Inclusion of Roma Localities has been working to promote partnerships between stakeholders at local level for the implementation of social inclusion projects...and is currently working in 23 localities and plans to start working in ten more localities in 2011...However, in an article from the Czech Roma news source Romae.cz, the Agency Director explains that the organization is leaving Brno, one of the pilot locations in which the Agency first started working, because it "has not been able to effectively intervene in the social inclusion process there or to guarantee the successful fulfillment of key measures....The Council of Europe's Commissioner for Human Rights, noting that the Czech Roma "continue to be particularly vulnerable to discrimination and racism", cites as still relevant his predecessor's 2006 report in which it was said that "many members of this community...remain caught in a spiral of exclusion and marginalization affecting practically all areas of life, from employment to housing, education and personal safety." The NUPI senior research fellow also says that in spite of the "numerous changes" implemented by the government, the situation of the Czech Roma "remains perilous in many respects". She explains that "Czech Roma are still largely segregated in terms of housing and education. A shadow report by the European Network Against Racism (ENAR) on racism and discrimination in the Czech Republic between 1 January 2009 and 31 March 2010 similarly notes that "Roma still remain the primary target of racist violence and institutional racism" and that "discrimination and stratification of Roma" in areas such as education, health, employment and housing "remain significant"..
Attacks against Roma in the Czech Republic: January 2008-December 2010
[17] In a report31 published by the European Roma Rights Centre, dated December 8, 2010, it states:
The following is a list of 17 attacks against Roma and/or their property in the Czech Republic between January 2008 and December 2010:
On May 14, 2010, unidentified perpetrators set a garbage can on fire and dragged it in front of the entrance of an apartment building in Rybarska street where several Romani families live. The perpetrators were allegedly young people, but were not identified. The police reportedly refused to investigate the incident. There were no injuries reported.....On April 30, 2010, unknown perpetrators attacked a Romani home in Uhersky Ostroh on 30 April with a Molotov cocktail. The explosive broke through a window and damaged some property. Residents managed to put out the fire and prevent the Molotov cocktail from exploding. There were no injuries reported....On April 3, 2010, unknown perpetrators attacked an apartment building in Rybarska street, Opava, at about 1:00 AM on 3 April, 2010. The perpetrators threw two bottles into the entranceway of the building and another Molotov cocktail was found in a nearby abandoned apartment on the first floor. A 55-year-old occupant reported the case to Opava police at 6:30 AM that same morning. Opava Police Commissioner Rene Cernohorsky was quoted as having stated: "The bottles never caught fire, no fire ever started, there were no injuries and no damages were caused."....On 29 March, 2010, four perpetrators shot an air rifle at Roma in the town of Karvina, hitting a nine-year-old girl in the thigh, her mother in the arm and their ethnic Czech neighbour in the collarbone...the victims called the police immediately after the incident and while the police were at the scene persons outside cursed the victims, calling them "black swine". Ladislav Bal of the European Roma CZ association was quoted as having stated that about 150 Roma gathered after the attack to defend the area against the shooters; police reportedly ended up protecting the assailants from the crowd...Over the weekend of 27 and 28 March 2010, three perpetrators attacked Police Commissioner Lubormir Horvath, a Romani man, in his apartment in Sokolov. As a result of the attack, Mr. Horvath's leg was fractured in three places and doctors had to insert screws into his leg. One of the perpetrators also chased his 19-year-old son, knocked him to the ground and kicked him while shouting "black swine". The police reportedly stated that the attack was racially , however, the state prosecutor refused to take the perpetrators into custody....On the morning of 14 March 2010 a Molotov cocktail was thrown into the home of a Romani family in Ostrava's Bedriska settlement...Police discovered the bottle contained ether indicating that the perpetrators may have intended those in the room to be rendered unconscious if the bottle did not catch fire immediately....A special 15-member team was investigating the attack as one of reckless endangerment. Police arrested the non-Romani neighbours of the family and charged two of them with the attempted murder. On May 28, 2009, unknown perpetrators attacked a Romani family in the village of Zdiby near Prague by throwing two Molotov cocktails at their home....The Praha-Venkov District Police Spokesperson, Zdenek Chalupa, stated that the initial damages were estimated at approximately 10,000 CZK and that the unidentified perpetrator was suspected of reckless endangerment; racial motivation was neither confirmed nor denied....On April 19, 2009, in an arson attack on a Romani house in Vitkov, northern Czech Republic, three Roma suffered serious injuries; among them a two-year-old girl who suffered burns to 80% of her body and had three of her fingers amputated....The police reportedly considered racial motivation to be possible due to witness accounts that something was thrown from a car which had stopped in front of the house while someone yelled "now you burn, Gypsies".....In February of 2009, a small group of ethnic Czech youth beat a 30-year-old Romani man when he intervened in an apparent disagreement between the youth and another young man....The suspects dropped a mobile phone at the scene, from which police reportedly retrieved fascist videos and other Nazi materials were found during a search of their home...On January 25, 2009, unknown perpetrators attacked a building inhabited by Romani people; the perpetrators threw two Molotov cocktails into the building...According to police spokeswoman Jitka Dolejsova, there were no injuries...On December 19, 2008, around a dozen right-wing supporters between the ages of 17 and 25 beat a 17-year-old Romani youth, causing partial paralysis. The police were reportedly treating this as a racist attack and had taken several suspects into custody...On November 17, 2008, a group of 500 neo-Nazis set off to march through Litvinov's Janov Romani neighbourhood. A strong police force with heavy equipment prevented the extremists from reaching the neighbourhood. During heavy clashes between police and the demonstrators, 14 people suffered injuries and three extremists were arrested. About 300 additional extremists were stopped in the nearby town of Most and the police discovered dozens of weapons in their cars. Several demonstrations by far-right wing groups took place in Litvinov and other locations in the Czech Republic in the following months, although some of them were banned by local municipalities and thwarted by counter-demonstrations organized by local Roma.
Analysis of Board's Documents
[18] I find that the documentary evidence above establishes that, despite the numerous measures, policies, and legislation that the Government of the Czech Republic has adopted and implemented with regard to its stated objectives of eliminating racial discrimination against the Romani population, protecting their rights, and improving their socio-economic status, there continues to be widespread discrimination against the Roma in the Czech Republic in the areas of employment, education, housing, and health care. According to the documentary evidence, in some instances, it is the municipal governments at the local level that have been frustrating the well-intentioned plans of the Central Czech Government to bring about social and economic equality for members of the Romani community. I find that the preponderance of the documentary evidence above establishes that the anti-discrimination measures, policies, and plans of the Central Czech Government have not been implemented effectively.
[19] I also acknowledge, based on my review of the above documentation, that anti-Roma sentiments and hate-speeches at marches and demonstrations organized by racist groups have become more prevalent of late. However, I find that the documentary evidence regarding the level of racially motivated acts of violence perpetrated by Neo-Nazis and extremists against the Roma is conflicting. According to some of the documentary sources of information above, there has been an increase in racially motivated attacks on the Roma in the Czech Republic. On the contrary, others documentary sources suggest that there has actually been a decline in the level of violence perpetrated by racists and extremists against the Roma since the early 1990's. The Department of State Reports on Human Rights Practices in the Czech Republic for 2009 and 2010, indicate that latent societal discrimination against the Romani population only occasionally manifested itself in violence. I find, given the lack of reliable statistical or empirical data regarding racially motivated attacks on members of the Roma community, that I am unable to find, either way, that violent attacks on the Roma have either increased or decreased. However, I find that the preponderance of the documentary evidence establishes, on a balance of probabilities, that members of the Romani population in the Czech Republic continue to be targets of racially motivated violence carried out by Neo-Nazis and national extremists.
[20] Based on my review of the documentary evidence above, relating to the level of response from police authorities in connection with racially motivated acts of violence against the Romani community, I find that the evidence is somewhat mixed. There is conflicting information depending on which source is consulted. For example, in Response to Information Request,32 the European Network against Racism describes police investigation of crimes committed against Roma citizens as inadequate. According to Holomek of the SRNM and to Miroslav Mares, a professor political science at Masaryk University in Brno, a different source, police response to racially motivated demonstrations targeting Roma is generally adequate. In contrast to this opinion, Vales of the Czech Helsinki Committee (CHV), described the police response to Neo-Nazi demonstrations as inadequate.
[21] However, based on my review of the preponderance of the documentary evidence above, more specifically my examination of the reported incidents involving attacks on the Roma by Neo-Nazis and racists in the Czech Republic, and the efforts made by the Czech Government and law enforcement agencies to combat extremism, I find that the level of protection afforded to Romani targets of racially motivated violence is adequate. I acknowledge, according to the documentary evidence, that there have been individual instances where police authorities have been non-responsive or unwilling to investigate attacks on Roma or have outright refused to assist Romani victims or shown indifference and disrespect towards Romani seeking assistance from police authorities. However, I find that there is no persuasive documentary evidence above that establishes that this problem is systemic or widespread or that police authorities, either at an institutional or operational level, routinely refuse to help Romani targets of violence or refuse to properly investigate crimes committed against members of the Romani community or refuse to prepare written reports about complaints made by Roma at police stations.
[22] On the contrary, I find, when I examine all of the reported cases of violent crimes committed against the Roma in different communities throughout the Czech Republic that the police response generally has been and continues to be adequate. In August of 2008, police arrested four men for throwing Molotov cocktails into the home of a Romani family in the town of Vitkov on April 19. In August of 2008, four Romani clients and non-Romani staff of a bar in the town of Rokycany were assaulted by a group of approximately 15 young people. Staff at the bar was attacked allegedly because they served Romani customers. Czech police denied the attack had been racially motivated. Five men were prosecuted for lesser crimes of disorderly conduct, delinquency and intention to cause bodily harm. On May 12, 2010, the regional court in Ostrava opened the trial against Jaromir Lukes, David Vaculi, Ivo Mueller, and Vaclav Cojocaru, who were accused of throwing Molotov cocktails into the home of a Romani family in the town of Vitkov in April of 2009. Vaculik, Mueller, and Lukes received 22-year sentences, and Cojocaru received a 20-year sentence. These are the longest sentences ever handed down for a racially motivated crime. In November 2008 an estimated 1,000 police officers used force to prevent an estimated 500 well-armed, right-wing rioters in Litvinov from attacking Roma. Starting October 1, 2009, the government stepped up police presence in Roma neighbourhoods, including an increase from 21 to 3 officers in Janov, the Litvinov Roma neighbourhood. The Czech Worker's Party had previously tried to organize similar demonstrations against the Romani community in Litvinov on October 4 and 18, 2008, and again on November 29, 2008, but had been banned by the local authorities. On April 4, 2009, far-right groups organized a march through the Romani district in the town of Prerov. About 400 demonstrators, chanting anti-Roma slogans and joined by local inhabitants, marched through the town and the Romani neighbourhood. Around 700 police officers prevented direct attacks on Roma. In October of 2009, the police arrested eight suspects accused of attacks on Roma in Havirov in November of 2008. The case was before the Regional Court in Ostrava at the end of 2009. In 2009, the Police arrested a number of right-wing extremists during the year. On June 9, 2009, the special police unit for investigating organized crime (UOOZ) charged 10 neo-Nazis with supporting and promoting movements aimed at suppressing the rights and freedoms of the individual. They remained in custody and their cases were pending at year's end. In June of 2010, the district court of Benesov found 11 of the defendant guilty, sentencing two individuals to 30 months imprisonment and one individual to two years' imprisonment. In 2009, eight men were on trial at year's end for a series of attacks on Roma in November 2008 in Sumbark, a neighbourhood in Havirov with a large Romani population. On December 17, 2010, the regional court in Ostrava found the defendants guilty. Over the past two years, 2009-2010, the police focused greater attention on combating promoters of extremism. The Security Information Service (BIS), the country's civilian domestic intelligence agency, reported at the start of May of 2010 that right-wing extremist activity decreased in the country during the first six months of the year. BIS stated that the developments involving neo-Nazis were particularly influenced by police raids in 2009 against members of extremist parties and the prohibition of the Workers' Party. As a result of a televised advertisement that referred to a "final solution of the Gypsy question", the Prague Municipal State Attorney's Office filed charges in September 2009 against a member of the unregistered, ultranationalist National Party for inciting hatred based on race. In November of 2009, a Prague district court gave a representative of the National Party a one-year suspended sentence and three year's probation for the advertisement. On February 17, 2010, the Supreme Administrative Court in Brno decided that a second motion filed by the Czech Government to dissolve the Worker's Party is grounds for the party to be dissolved.33 In the opinion, presiding judge Vojtech Simicek mentioned several points of the party's program which contravene the law. According to the opinion, the party is "populist, homophobic, chauvinistic, and demonstrates racist tendencies. The court also evaluated the content of the party's campaign ads, which Czech Radio refused to broadcast due to their references to "Gypsies", as the party calls Roma. The court found that the ads were racist as they passed judgment on the Roma ethnicity as a whole. While the court recognized that many of the topics raised by the party are actual social problems, generalizations about a particular group of people on the basis of their ethnicity are unacceptable under the law. The party defines itself through generalizing about a particular group of people on the basis of their ethnicity are unacceptable under the law. The party defines itself through generalizing about the Jewish, Roma, Vietnamese and other minorities, as well as about homosexuals, immigrants, etc, creating the impression that the majority is threatened by the existence of these minorities. According to the Supreme Administrative Court, the party attacks the very basis of the democratic state system. On March 14, 2010, a Molotov cocktail was thrown into the home of a Romani family in Ostrava's Bedriska settlement. A special 15-member team was investigating the attack as one of reckless endangerment. Police arrested the non-Romani neighbours of the family and charged two of them with attempted murder.
Male Claimant's Documents
Internet Article: "Persecution of Roma in the Czech Republic", September 9, 2011
[23] In an internet article34 published by World Socialist Website, it states:
In recent weeks, the Czech Republic has witnessed violent attacks on the Roma minority, sparked by clashes between Roma and young Czechs. The ethnic tensions are a direct consequence of increasing social decline. As in Hungary and other European countries, bourgeois and far-right parties are exploiting the discriminated and disadvantaged minority in an effort to channel social tensions along racist lines...The neo-fascist Workers Party (DS) has called a rally in Novy Bor for September 10. In the past, activities involving the DS often ended violently. Roma representative Albert Deme ter recently spoke to Czech television about the "hunting down of Roma", which he compared to "the days of fascism"...
Article: "Anti-Roma Protests Turn Violent in the Czech Republic"
[24] In an internet article35 published by Spiegel Online, dated September 16, 2011, it states:
For weeks there have been riots between Czech locals and newly settled Roma in northern Bohemia. What started as a series of brutal but isolated fights has grown into a popular movement in small towns along the eastern German border. Right-wing extremists have fanned the hatred...
Report: "Hate Crimes and Victim Assistance in the Czech Republic"
[25] In a report36 published by Foundation EVZ, it states:
However, regional and internet media in particular often use, and as a result support, public animosity against Roma in their news reports, and especially in criminal cases they emphasise the ethnicity of the perpetrators....In the opinion of the respondents, there is a general lack of trust in the work of the police among their clients and marginalized groups in the Czech Republic. This has its roots in both the personal experience of these persons, as well as in the experiences of their friends and acquaintances....Another cause is the unwillingness or the inability of the police to spend time on these clients' cases. This is not only true of hate violence cases-there is a certain degree of mistrust of the police in the general public as well...It is not just the excess of work, however, that can lead individual police officers to try to brush aside certain cases that to them seem insignificant. A large part of the criticism is caused by the police's lack of interest, and by their inability to put themselves in the victim's place.....
Report: "State Response to Violence against Roma"
[26] In a report from the European Roma Rights Centre37, dated February 14, 2012, it states:
A recent European Union Survey on Minorities and Discrimination highlights that on average one in five Roma respondents were victims of racially motivated personal crime at least once in the previous 12 months. 81% of Roma who indicated they were victims of assault, threat or serious harassment considered that their victimization was racially motivated. Between 65% and 100% of Roma in the surveyed European countries did not report their experiences of personal victimization to the police. The main reason given by Roma was that they were not confident that the police would be able to do anything...The fact is that racist or stigmatising anti-Roma rhetoric has been on the rise both in public and political discourse, including accusations that Roma as an ethnic group are engaged in criminal behaviour....In some cases, these words were understood as encouraging violent action against the Roma, such as mob riots or violence...Anti-Gypsy stereotypes also continue to be spread and perpetuated in the media across Europe, which report primarily on Roma in the context of social problems and crime. Violence against Roma remains a serious problem not only because it harms the Roma directly affected by the attack, but because the Roma as an ethnic group are impacted by the lack of an effective response by state authorities. In 2011, the European Roma Rights Centre published a report examining the state response to 44 selected violent attacks against Roma in the Czech Republic, Hungary, and Slovakia. A number of shortcomings in the state response to violence against Roma are apparent. Many Romani victims of violent crimes do not secure justice. A limited number of perpetrators of violent attacks against Roma are successfully identified, investigated, and prosecuted. Even fewer are eventually imprisoned for the crimes they have committed against Roma...The failure of law enforcement authorities to identify the perpetrators of crimes against Roma in a considerable number of investigations creates a climate of impunity and may encourage further acts of violence against Roma. The issuance by courts of only suspended prison sentences to persons found guilty of serious crimes against Roma reinforces the message that it is ok to attack Roma....."
Analysis of the Male claimant's documentation
[27] Based on my detailed review of the supporting country documents provided by the male claimant through his counsel, Grace Wang, I find that the preponderance of their documents clearly establish that despite the anti-discrimination measures, including the passage by the Czech Government of a comprehensive Anti-Discrimination Act in June of 2009, and the creation of government programs aimed at bringing about social inclusion and integration of the Roma into mainstream Czech society and helping Roma to substantially improve their level of education, their standard of living, and access to employment opportunities, there continues to be at the present time significant and widespread societal discrimination against the Romani people in the Czech Republic in education, employment, housing, and health care.
[28] In terms of the level of protection afforded to members of the Romani community in the Czech Republic from law enforcement agencies, including the police, I find that the information found in the documentary evidence presented by the male claimants is somewhat mixed. According to one source, in the male claimant's documents, when a thousand people surrounded homes inhabited by Roma, seeking to provoke them, the police intervened when people, neo-Nazis among them, began to demolish a fence. The same source indicates that one Roma family, after unsuccessfully appealing to the local police after they were threatened, had to flee the area. In another news report, it indicated that during a major riot against the Roma in Bohemia, there was a massive police presence and that around 600 police officers, many in riot gear, tried to keep the situation under control. Another source makes reference to incidents in 2006 involving threats and attacks on Roma by neo-Nazis in which there was a timely and swift response by police authorities resulting in the arrest of the culprits. Yet another source indicates that the response by the Czech authorities to protect Roma facing violent retribution and attacks by racists has been largely ineffective. Another source indicates that the Czech authorities have failed to properly investigate and identify racist perpetrators who have been responsible for acts of violence committed against members of the Romani community. This same source indicates that only a limited number of perpetrators of violent attacks against Roma have been successfully identified, investigated, and prosecuted.
[29] I acknowledge, based on my review of all of the documentary evidence contained in the Board's disclosure package referred to above, and all of the documentary submitted by the male claimant through his counsel, Grace Wang, that ethnic Romani people in the Czech Republic face at the present time widespread and significant discrimination in the areas of education, housing, employment, and health care. However, I find that the documentary evidence as a whole does not establish that the acts of discrimination that ethnic Roma face in the Czech Republic rise to level of serious harm amounting to persecution either individually or cumulatively. Although I accept that, according to the documentary evidence, ethnic Roma face significant discrimination in the areas referred to above, I do not find any persuasive evidence to show that the discrimination that ethnic Roma face generally in these areas poses a serious threat to their fundamental human rights amounting to persecution, i.e. that this discrimination rises to a level of serious harm to their lives or their physical integrity or safety or that that discrimination gives rise to consequences of a substantially prejudicial nature amounting to serious harm to their fundamental human rights.
[30] I also acknowledge that there are documentary sources referred to above which are diametrically opposed and contradictory to one another in terms of their assessment of the availability or accessible of adequate state protection to members of the Romani minority in the Czech Republic. Some of the reports indicate that the level of protection available to the members of the Romani community who are subjected to racially motivated threats and violence is totally inadequate. Other reports indicate that the level of police response to racially motivated attacks against members of the Romani community has been and continues to be adequate. Some of the documentary sources suggest that violence against ethnic Roma at the hands of extremists and neo-Nazis is on the rise, while yet other reports suggest that there has been no statistically significant increase in the number of violent acts perpetrated against the Roma by extremists and neo-Nazis in the Czech Republic. Other reports indicate that discrimination and racism against the Roma only "occasionally" manifests itself in violent acts against the Roma.
[31] Having carefully considered all of the documentary reports and sources referred to above from the Board and from the male claimant's counsel, Grace Wang, I find that the preponderance of the documentary evidence establishes that the level of police response in connection with racially motivated acts of violence against the Roma and that the protection afforded members of the Romani community from law enforcement and the judicial system and criminal justice system in connection with acts of violence and threats of violence against the Romani community in the Czech Republic has generally been adequate. I do acknowledge, however, that there have been instances were police officers and law enforcement officials have treated Roma with disrespect and have at times resorted to the use of violence against ethnic Roma. I also acknowledge that there have been individual instances where members of the Czech Republic police have acted negligently in responding to complaints from ethnic Roma and that there have been instances where police officers and law enforcement officials have failed to investigate crimes committed against the Roma or have conducted shoddy investigations into incidents in which ethnic Roma have been threatened or subjected to violence at the hands of extremists and neo-Nazis. I also acknowledge that the central government of the Czech Republic has faced and continues to face significant challenges with respect to the implementation of anti-discrimination and anti-racism legislation and programs at the local level and that at there appears to be a patch work of inconsistent implementation of these programs by municipal governments in different regions and parts of the country. However, I find that the documentary evidence, as a whole, does not establish a pattern of conduct on the part of the police, law enforcement agencies, and the criminal justice system is demonstrative of a failure of the state of the Czech Republic to provide ethnic Roma with adequate state protection.
Analysis-Male Claimant's interactions with the Police-Czech Republic
[32] In this particular case, the male claimant was questioned as to what efforts he made in seeking state protection before fleeing the Czech Republic.
[33] In 2004, when the male claimant was living in the city of XXXXX at his parent's home, his father asked him to go to a store to buy him some cigarettes. When he was walking along the street, he saw a group of men with shaved heads and camouflage clothing approaching him. They jumped on the male claimant and beat him up. He sustained bruises all over his body and a black eye. The male claimant went home and called the police on the phone. He told the police he wanted to report the attack and the police asked him if he knew who his attackers were. When the male claimant told the police that he did not know them, the police told him that there was nothing they could do to help. The male claimant's father argued with the police over the phone and insisted that they do something but realized that it was pointless. The police told the male claimant's father that because they could not give them a description of the men who attacked the male claimant, there was nothing they could do about it and therefore they could not investigate it. The male claimant indicated that the attack happened very quickly and that as a result he could not describe his attackers to the police. The male claimant was asked if he went to see the police in person to make a complaint about his attack. He said that he did not go to see the person in person because it was a waste of time. It was brought to the attention of the male claimant that in his PIF narrative, it indicated that he had gone to see the police in person. He said that he was very nervous, had not slept all night, and that the incident had happened a long time ago and therefore he could not remember very well. The male claimant was asked if he could remember if there were any eye witnesses when he was attacked. He said he could not remember. I find, given that the police had very limited information to go on, that there apparently were no eye witnesses, and the male claimant could not describe his attackers to the police, that the response was adequate. I accept the male claimant's explanation that he did not approach the police in person after he was attacked and spoke to the police over the phone. I find, in these circumstances, that it is not reasonable to expect that the police would be able to solve this crime with ease or that they would not face significant investigative or evidentiary challenges in trying to look into this matter.
[34] In 2005 or 2006, the male claimant moved out of his parent's home in XXXXX city and moved in with his common-law partner in XXXX town. In 2006, the male claimant's daughter was born. In 2008, after the male claimant visited his parents in XXXXX city, he was on his way home, when he was confronted by a group of 4 skinheads. The skinheads started calling him names and chasing him. He began to panic and started running away from the skinheads. They caught up with him and beat him up severely. The claimant was admitted to a hospital in XXXXX city. The male claimant reported the attack to the police but he could not remember if the police came to see him at the hospital or if he went to see them in person. The police asked the male claimant if he knew his attackers and wanted to know if he provoked them. The police told the male claimant that they were not going to investigate and told him that he was the perpetrator and the one who attacked the four skinheads. The police threatened the male claimant by telling him to withdraw his statement or else they were going to make trouble for him because he probably was the one who carried out the attack. The male claimant then decided it was pointless to pursue any kind of complaint. The male claimant was asked if he made inquiries following his 2004 and 2008 attack to find out if there were any government agencies where he could make a complaint about his unfair treatment by the police. The male claimant said that he did not do so because it was impossible for gypsies to complain about police misconduct and that even if a gypsy did complain about the police they would get nowhere. The male claimant gave the example of his brother who was assaulted by a police officer during a routine traffic check because he did not have his driver's licence with him. He said his brother was hospitalized and that after he got out of the hospital he received a document from the police threatening him that if he did not withdraw his complaint the police would charge him. I find that the male claimant has not furnished any persuasive evidence to show that it is impossible for members of the Roma community to make complaints about police misconduct in the Czech Republic.
[35] I find, in light of the evidence above, that the male claimant did not take all reasonable steps, in the circumstances of this case, in seeking the protection and assistance of police authorities and government agencies before fleeing the Czech Republic and coming to Canada. I find that the male claimant has not furnished any persuasive evidence to show that it was impossible for him to complain about what he and his father perceived to be unfair police treatment when he and his father attempted to make a report concerning the attacks that he suffered in 2004 and 2008. Despite what the male claimant perceived to be unfair treatment by the police in 2004 and 2008, he did not make any efforts or inquiries to find out if he could lodge a complaint about his unfair treatment by police with any government agencies.
[36] Having taken all the evidence into consideration, I find that the male claimant has not produced sufficient reliable, probative, and convincing evidence to persuade me, on a balance of probabilities, that the level of protection available to him in practice, as a member of the Romani minority, from police authorities and law enforcement agencies in the Czech Republic, in response to racially motivated threats of violence or actual violence at the hands of skinheads, is inadequate.
[37] I find, in the circumstances of this case, that the male claimant has not rebutted the legal presumption of adequate state protection in the Czech Republic with clear and convincing evidence. Therefore, I am not persuaded that the Czech Republic would not be reasonably forthcoming with adequate state protection, should the male claimant seek it.
[38] Therefore, I find the male claimant is not a Convention refugee and his claim under section 96 of the IRPA fails. The rest of the analysis of this decision will be under section 97 of the IRPA.
Section 97 - Persons in Need of Protection
State Protection
[39] For the same reasons, as set out above, I find that the male claimant is not a person in need of protection. I find that he is not a person in need of protection because adequate state protection would be available to him in practice in the Czech Republic were he to return there today.
[40] Therefore, I find the male claimant is not a person in need of protection and his claim under section 97 of the IRPA fails.
Claim of Common-law partner and daughter
[41] As the claims of his common-law partner and daughter are entirely dependent upon the claim of the male claimant, and there is no persuasive evidence before me to substantially differentiate their claims from his, their claims under section 96 and 97 of the IRPA also fail.
CONCLUSION
[42] I find that the claimants are neither Convention refugees nor persons in need of protection. The Refugee Protection Division therefore rejects their claims.
(signed)
"Robert Bafaro"
Robert Bafaro
May 14, 2012
Date
